We turned the Big Ideas 2026 report into a free 13-lesson course, and it just launched on Interactive Brokers @IBKR.

Here is some of what investors will learn: 
→ How technological convergence is driving a Great Acceleration and why we are still early 
→ How AI infrastructure, productivity, and the consumer operating system are reshaping the economy 
→ Why Bitcoin is maturing into an institutional asset 
→ What decentralized finance applications are doing in 2026 
→ How multiomics is transforming healthcare from treatment to prevention 
→ Why reusable rockets are unlocking a new space economy 
→ What humanoid robotics means for global productivity 
→ How distributed energy is powering the next cycle of economic growth 
→ Why autonomous vehicles and logistics could be the biggest cost reduction in transportation history

Free to access, CFA Accredited, and built for any investor ready to understand what comes next.

Interested? Start the course now! ⬇️

tweets.text:
Intentionally mislead much?
>DLR delivered 490+ MW gross in 2024–2025 per their earnings calls, not 162 MW net.
>You used net in-service MW change, missing dispositions and retirements. At $10M/MW, 490+ MW of gross deliveries = $4.9B+ of growth capex, consuming essentially all of the $6B total.
>DLR discloses recurring capex explicitly: ~$627M over two years on ~2,100 MW = ~$149K/MW/year. That's 7x below your assumption on a line item they literally break out every quarter.

thread_tweets.text:
Rising construction costs over 20 years will make WULF's already-built $11M/MW campus harder to replicate, not less valuable. Replacement cost inflation is a moat for existing asset owners. 
And none of this affects a dollar of the contracted $950M/yr lease revenue.
The investment thesis is the cash flows, not the book value or PP&E duh.

thread_tweets.text:
I think the real issue with the $DLR $EQIX comparison is lease duration. Those REITs average ~5-year lease terms. Every cycle, they re-commission space, install second-generation tenant improvements, pay leasing commissions, and retrofit for new power density. DLR discloses this explicitly as "recurring capex" in their filings.

$WULF has a 20-year single-tenant Anthropic lease. None of that turnover capex exists for two decades.

tweets.text: The US sold $39 billion of 10-year notes at the highest yield since February 2025, at 4.58%. There was strong demand from investors, but they're demanding a relatively high level of compensation to own the debt. (February 2025 was before the Fed cut rates by 75bp.) https://t.co/w0pTrn4pYV
